title: Topological fragility and bilinear magnetoelectric resistance in gapless edge states
authors: Cosimo Gorini, Matthieu Bard, Sophie Gueron, Hélène Bouchiat, Giovanni Vignale
abstract: In time-reversal symmetric systems such as topological and higher-order topological insulators, 1D spin-momentum locked edge and hinge states are theoretically ``perfectly conducting'', being immune to backscattering by non-magnetic disorder. Here, we reveal a fundamental ``topological fragility'': these states exhibit a bilinear magnetoelectric resistance significantly larger than in 2D systems. This effect requires two ingredients: (i) spin-momentum locking, which maximizes time-reversal symmetry breaking in the non-linear regime, and (ii) random spin-orbit interaction -- the same mechanism behind Elliott - Yafet spin relaxation in heavy elements. Together, these generate a robust backscattering channel when a modest external magnetic field is applied. Our theory requires no gap opening or complex many-body effects, offering a simple and general mechanism that quantitatively explains recent observations in Bismuth hinge states.
summary: This paper resolves the paradox of how perfectly conducting 1D topological states exhibit resistance. It demonstrates that the same spin-orbit coupling providing topological protection also enables backscattering when an external magnetic field breaks time-reversal symmetry. This 'topological fragility' explains recent experimental observations in Bismuth nanowires.
main_problem: Explaining the origin of Bilinear Magnetoelectric Resistance (BMER) in 1D spin-momentum locked edge and hinge states, which contradicts the theoretical expectation of perfect conductivity due to immunity to backscattering.
main_result: The authors identify a 'topological fragility' where random spin-orbit interaction enables a backscattering channel when time-reversal symmetry is broken by an external magnetic field, providing a quantitative explanation for BMER in Bismuth hinge states.
method: The study uses a theoretical framework involving the Landauer-Büttiker formulation, Fermi's Golden Rule for scattering rates, and a gauge transformation to analyze non-reciprocal transport.
model_or_system: The model consists of 1D Dirac-type edge or hinge states (e.g., in Bismuth) characterized by spin-momentum locking, subject to a Hamiltonian with an external magnetic field and random spin-orbit interaction (Elliott-Yafet mechanism).
key_observables: Bilinear Magnetoelectric Resistance (BMER), non-reciprocal transport, reflection coefficient, and current-induced magnetization.
important_parameters: Spin-orbit length (lambda), Fermi velocity (vF), magnetic field strength (H), impurity density (ni), and the ratio of impurity energy fluctuations to Fermi energy (epsilon_0/epsilon_F).
main_assumptions: Assumes weak backscattering, delta-correlated disorder, and that the system equilibrates in the presence of a magnetic field before current is driven; neglects complex many-body effects.
figures_summary: Figure 3(a) shows an experimental setup of Bi nanowires; Figure 4 illustrates Fermi level densities for different Dirac dispersions; Figure 5 shows energy dispersion with and without magnetic fields.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the paradox of BMER in protected states, introduces a model for random spin-orbit interaction, derives the backscattering mechanism via the Elliott-Yafet process, compares exchange vs. magneto-static effects, and concludes with quantitative agreement to experimental data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematics of a spin-momentum locked 1D wire con- necting reservoirs at chemical potentials µ+,µ−. The magne- tizing field H forms an angle φ with the channel, while the electronic spins are parallel to the spin-momentum locking di- rection ˆm. The net current I(φ) is different under positive (δµ > 0) and negative (δµ < 0) bias, with δµ ≡µ+−µ−. The asymmetry is quantified by the resistance RBMER, Eq. (15).

FIG. 3. (a): Sketch of the experimental setup. The shape of the Bi nanowire defines multiple hinges of either helicities, whose spin-momentum locking direction ˆm forms an angle of 65◦with the nanowire axis. An external field is applied in the wire plane at an angle θ. The imposed total current is IAC = 2.5µA, through a wire of diameter d ∼100nm. See Ref. [17] for further details. (b) BMER traces for varying θ, corresponding to an angle between magnetic field and spin axis φ = 65◦−θ. The data were antisymmetrized to isolate the rectifying (2nd harmonic in current bias) odd-in-B com- ponent, excluding B-even contributions. Data points at small fields B ≲0.2T, see inset, are perfectly fitted...
FIG. 4. Fermi level densities of injected right- (+) and left- movers (-) for linear (blue) and cubic (red) Dirac dispersions.

FIG. 5. Left panel: Energy dispersion for the model Hamiltonian vF pσx. The right movers with p > 0 and positive spin along the x axis have the same energy as their time-reversed left-moving partners with opposite momentum and spin. The short horizontal lines are the chemical potentials, which determine the occupations of right and left movers in the non-equilibrium state. The elastic back scattering process shown by the arrow is forbidden by time-reversal symmetry. Right panel: Energy dispersion for the model Hamiltonian (vF p + gµ0µBH)σx. Right- and left-moving states at the same energy do not have opposite momenta and spins, i.e. they are not time-reversal partners. Elastic back...
